volumeTitle: Children of Heracles. Hippolytus. Andromache. Hecuba
label: Hippolytus (Section 599 - Section 2.755)

sections.label: Section 599
sections.original: οὐκ οἶδα πλὴν ἕν · κατθανεῖν ὅσον τάχος ,
sections.translation: I only know one way ,

sections.label: Section 600
sections.original: τῶν νῦν παρόντων πημάτων ἄκος μόνον .
sections.translation: one cure for these my woes and that is instant death .

sections.label: Section 601
sections.original: ὦ γαῖα μῆτερ ἡλίου τ ’ ἀναπτυχαί ,
sections.translation: O mother earth ! O sun ’ s unclouded orb ! What words , unfit for any lips , have reached my ears !

sections.label: Section 603
sections.original: σίγησον , ὦ παῖ , πρίν τιν ’ αἰσθέσθαι βοῆς .
sections.translation: Peace , my son , lest some one hear thy outcry .

sections.label: Section 604
sections.original: οὐκ ἔστ ’ ἀκούσας δείν ’ ὅπως σιγήσομαι .
sections.translation: I cannot hear such awful words and hold my peace .

sections.label: Section 605
sections.original: ναὶ πρός σε τῆς σῆς δεξιᾶς εὐωλένου .
sections.translation: I do implore thee by thy fair right hand .

sections.label: Section 606
sections.original: οὐ μὴ προσοίσεις χεῖρα μηδ ’ ἅψῃ πέπλων .
sections.translation: Let go my hand , touch not my robe .

sections.label: Section 607
sections.original: ὦ πρός σε γονάτων , μηδαμῶς μ ’ ἐξεργάσῃ .
sections.translation: O by thy knees I pray , destroy me not utterly .

sections.label: Section 608
sections.original: τί δ ’, εἴπερ , ὡς φῄς , μηδὲν εἴρηκας κακόν ;
sections.translation: Why say this , if , as thou pretendest , thy lips are free from blame ?

sections.label: Section 609
sections.original: ὁ μῦθος , ὦ παῖ , κοινὸς οὐδαμῶς ὅδε .
sections.translation: My son , this is no story to be noised abroad .

sections.label: Section 610
sections.original: τά τοι κάλ ’ ἐν πολλοῖσι κάλλιον λέγειν .
sections.translation: A virtuous tale grows fairer told to many .

sections.label: Section 611
sections.original: ὦ τέκνον , ὅρκους μηδαμῶς ἀτιμάσῃς .
sections.translation: Never dishonour thy oath , thy son .

sections.label: Section 612
sections.original: ἡ γλῶσσ ’ ὀμώμοχ ’, ἡ δὲ φρὴν ἀνώμοτος .
sections.translation: My tongue an oath did take , but not my heart .

sections.label: Section 613
sections.original: ὦ παῖ , τί δράσεις ; σοὺς φίλους διεργάσῃ ;
sections.translation: My son , what wilt thou do ? destroy thy friends ?

sections.label: Section 614
sections.original: ἀπέπτυσ ’· οὐδεὶς ἄδικός ἐστί μοι φίλος .
sections.translation: Friends indeed ! the wicked are no friends of mine .

sections.label: Section 615
sections.original: σύγγνωθ ’· ἁμαρτεῖν εἰκὸς ἀνθρώπους , τέκνον .
sections.translation: O pardon me ; to err is only human , child .

sections.label: Section 616
sections.original: ὦ Ζεῦ , τί δὴ κίβδηλον ἀνθρώποις κακὸν
sections.translation: Great Zeus , why didst thou , to man ’ s sorrow , put woman , evil counterfeit , to dwell where shines the sun ? If thou wert minded that the human race should multiply , it was not from women they should have drawn their stock ,

sections.label: Section 620
sections.original: ἀλλ ’ ἀντιθέντας σοῖσιν ἐν ναοῖς βροτοὺς
sections.translation: but in thy temples they should have paid gold or iron or ponderous bronze and bought a family , each man proportioned to his offering , and so in independence dwelt , from women free .

sections.label: Section 625
sections.original: νῦν δ ’ ἐς δόμους μὲν πρῶτον ἄξεσθαι κακὸν
sections.translation: But now as soon as ever we would bring this plague into our home we bring its fortune to the ground . 1 [*] ’ Tis clear from this how great a curse a woman is ; the very father , that begot and nurtured her , to rid him of the mischief gives her a dower and packs her off ;

sections.label: Section 630
sections.original: ὁ δ ’ αὖ λαβὼν ἀτηρὸν ἐς δόμους φυτὸν
sections.translation: while the husband , who takes the noxious weed into his home , fondly decks his sorry idol in fine raiment and tricks her out in robes , squandering by degrees , unhappy wight ! his house ’ s wealth . For he is in this dilemma ;

sections.label: Section 635
sections.original: γαμβροῖσι χαίρων σῴζεται πικρὸν λέχος ,
sections.translation: say his marriage has brought him good connections , he is glad then to keep the wife he loathes ; or , if he gets a good wife but useless relations , he tries to stifle the bad luck with the good . But it is easiest for him who has settled in his house as wife a mere nobody , [*] incapable from simplicity .

sections.label: Section 640
sections.original: σοφὴν δὲ μισῶ · μὴ γὰρ ἔν γ ’ ἐμοῖς δόμοις
sections.translation: I hate a clever woman ; never may she set foot in my house who aims at knowing more than women need ; for in these clever women Cypris implants a larger store of villainy , while the artless woman is by her shallow wit from levity debarred .

sections.label: Section 645
sections.original: χρῆν δ ’ ἐς γυναῖκα πρόσπολον μὲν οὐ περᾶν ,
sections.translation: No servant should ever have had access to a wife , but men should put to live with them beasts , which bite , not talk , in which case they could not speak to any one nor be answered back by them . But , as it is , the wicked in their chambers plot wickedness ,

sections.label: Section 650
sections.original: βουλεύματ ’, ἔξω δ ’ ἐκφέρουσι πρόσπολοι .
sections.translation: and their servants carry it abroad .

sections.label: Section 651
sections.original: ὡς καὶ σύ γ ’ ἡμῖν πατρός , ὦ κακὸν κάρα ,
sections.translation: Even thus , vile wretch , thou cam ’ st to make me partner in an outrage on my father ’ s honour ; wherefore I must wash that stain away in running streams , dashing the water into my ears . How could I commit so foul a crime

sections.label: Section 655
sections.original: ὃς οὐδ ’ ἀκούσας τοιάδ ’ ἁγνεύειν δοκῶ ;
sections.translation: when by the very mention of it I feel myself polluted ? Be well assured , woman , ’ tis only my religious scruple saves thee . For had not I unawares been caught by an oath , ’ fore heaven ! I would not have refrained from telling all unto my father . But now I will from the house away , so long as

sections.label: Section 660
sections.original: Θησεύς , ἄπειμι · σῖγα δ ’ ἕξομεν στόμα .
sections.translation: Theseus is abroad , and will maintain strict silence . But , when my father comes , I will return and see how thou and thy mistress face him , and so shall I learn by experience the extent of thy audacity .

sections.label: Section 664
sections.original: ὄλοισθε . μισῶν δ ’ οὔποτ ’ ἐμπλησθήσομαι
sections.translation: Perdition seize you both ! ( To the audience ). I can never satisfy my hate for

sections.label: Section 665
sections.original: γυναῖκας , οὐδ ’ εἴ φησί τίς μ ’ ἀεὶ λέγειν ·
sections.translation: women , no ! not even though some say this is ever my theme , for of a truth they always are evil . So either let some one prove them chaste , or let me still trample on them for ever .

sections.label: Section 1.669
sections.original: τάλανες ὦ κακοτυχεῖς
sections.translation: O the cruel , unhappy fate of women !

sections.label: Section 1.670
sections.original: τίνα νῦν ἢ τέχναν ἔχομεν ἢ τίνας
sections.translation: What arts , what arguments have we , once we have made a slip , to loose by craft [*] the tight-drawn knot ? I have met my deserts . O earth , O light of day ! How can I escape the stroke of fate ? How my pangs conceal , kind friends ?

sections.label: Section 1.675
sections.original: τίς ἂν θεῶν ἀρωγὸς ἢ τίς ἂν βροτῶν
sections.translation: What god will appear to help me , what mortal to take my part or help me in unrighteousness ? The present calamity of my life admits of no escape . Most hapless I of all my sex !

sections.label: Section 1.680
sections.original: φεῦ φεῦ · πέπρακται , κοὐ κατώρθωνται τέχναι ,
sections.translation: Alas , alas ! the deed is done , thy servant ’ s schemes have gone awry , my queen , and all is lost .

sections.label: Section 1.682
sections.original: ὦ παγκακίστη καὶ φίλων διαφθορεῦ ,
sections.translation: Accursed woman ! traitress to thy friends ! How hast thou ruined me ! May Zeus , my ancestor , smite thee with his fiery bolt and uproot thee from thy place .

sections.label: Section 1.685
sections.original: οὐκ εἶπον — οὐ σῆς προυνοησάμην φρενός ; —
sections.translation: Did I not foresee thy purpose , did I not bid thee keep silence on the very matter which is now my shame ? But thou wouldst not be still ; wherefore my fair name will not go with me to the tomb . But now I must another scheme devise . Yon youth , in the keenness of his fury ,

sections.label: Section 1.690
sections.original: ἐρεῖ καθ ’ ἡμῶν πατρὶ σὰς ἁμαρτίας ,
sections.translation: will tell his father of my sin , and the aged Pittheus of my state , and fill the world with stories to my shame . Perdition seize thee and every meddling fool who by dishonest means would serve unwilling friends !

sections.label: Section 1.695
sections.original: δέσποιν ’, ἔχεις μὲν τἀμὰ μέμψασθαι κακά ·
sections.translation: Mistress , thou may ’ st condemn the mischief I have done , for sorrow ’ s sting o ’ ermasters thy judgment ; yet can I answer thee in face of this , if thou wilt hear . ’ Twas I who nurtured thee ; I love thee still ; but in my search for medicine to cure thy sickness I found what least I sought .

sections.label: Section 1.700
sections.original: εἰ δ ’ εὖ γ ’ ἔπραξα , κάρτ ’ ἂν ἐν σοφοῖσιν ἦ ·
sections.translation: Had I but succeeded , I had been counted wise , for the credit we get for wisdom is measured by our success .

sections.label: Section 1.702
sections.original: ἦ γὰρ δίκαια ταῦτα κἀξαρκοῦντά μοι ,
sections.translation: Is it just , is it any satisfaction to me , that thou shouldst wound me first , then bandy [*] words with me ?

sections.label: Section 1.704
sections.original: μακρηγοροῦμεν · οὐκ ἐσωφρόνουν ἐγώ .
sections.translation: We dwell on this too long ; I was not wise , I own ;

sections.label: Section 1.705
sections.original: ἀλλ ’ ἔστι κἀκ τῶνδ ’ ὥστε σωθῆναι , τέκνον .
sections.translation: but there are yet ways of escape from the trouble , my child .

sections.label: Section 1.706
sections.original: παῦσαι λέγουσα · καὶ τὰ πρὶν γὰρ οὐ καλῶς
sections.translation: Be dumb henceforth ; evil was thy first advice to me , evil too thy attempted scheme . Begone and leave me , look to thyself ; I will my own fortunes for the best arrange . ( Exit Nurse ).

sections.label: Section 1.710
sections.original: ὑμεῖς δέ , παῖδες εὐγενεῖς Τροζήνιαι ,
sections.translation: Ye noble daughters of Troezen , grant me the only boon I crave ; in silence bury what ye here have heard .

sections.label: Section 1.713
sections.original: ὄμνυμι σεμνὴν Ἄρτεμιν , Διὸς κόρην ,
sections.translation: By majestic Artemis , child of Zeus , I swear I will never divulge aught of thy sorrows .

sections.label: Section 1.715
sections.original: καλῶς ἔλεξας · ἓν δὲ προτρέπουσ ’ ἐγὼ
sections.translation: ’ Tis well . But I , with all my thought , [*] can but one way discover out of this calamity , that so I may secure my children ’ s honour , and find myself some help as matters stand . For never , never will I bring shame upon my Cretan home ,

sections.label: Section 1.720
sections.original: οὐδ ’ ἐς πρόσωπον Θησέως ἀφίξομαι
sections.translation: nor will I , to save one poor life , face Theseus after my disgrace .

sections.label: Section 1.722
sections.original: μέλλεις δὲ δὴ τί δρᾶν ἀνήκεστον κακόν ;
sections.translation: Art thou bent then on some cureless woe ?

sections.label: Section 1.723
sections.original: θανεῖν · ὅπως δέ , τοῦτ ’ ἐγὼ βουλεύσομαι .
sections.translation: On death ; the means thereto must I devise myself .

sections.label: Section 1.724
sections.original: εὔφημος ἴσθι .
sections.translation: Hush !

sections.label: Section 1.724b
sections.original: καὶ σύ γ ’ εὖ με νουθέτει .
sections.translation: Do thou at least advise me well .

sections.label: Section 1.725
sections.original: ἐγὼ δὲ Κύπριν , ἥπερ ἐξόλλυσί με ,
sections.translation: For this very day shall I gladden Cypris , my destroyer , by yielding up my life , and shall own myself vanquished by cruel love . Yet shall my dying be another ’ s curse , that he may learn not to exult at my misfortunes ;

sections.label: Section 1.730
sections.original: ὑψηλὸς εἶναι · τῆς νόσου δὲ τῆσδέ μοι
sections.translation: but when he comes to share the self-same plague with me , he will take a lesson in wisdom .

sections.label: Section 1.732
sections.original: Ἠλιβάτοις ὑπὸ κευθμῶσι γενοίμαν ,
sections.translation: O to be nestling ’ neath some pathless cavern , there by god ’ s creating hand to grow into a bird amid the winged tribes !

sections.label: Section 1.735
sections.original: ἀρθείην δ ’ ἐπὶ πόντιον
sections.translation: Away would I soar to Adrians wave-beat shore and to the waters of Eridanus ; where a father ’ s hapless daughters [*] in their grief for Phaethon

sections.label: Section 1.740
sections.original: κτῳ δακρύων
sections.translation: distil into the glooming flood the amber brilliance of their tears .

sections.label: Section 1.742
sections.original: Ἑσπερίδων δ ’ ἐπὶ μηλόσπορον ἀκτὰν
sections.translation: And to the apple-bearing strand of those minstrels in the west I then would come , where ocean ’ s lord

sections.label: Section 1.745
sections.original: ναύταις οὐκέθ ’ ὁδὸν νέμει ,
sections.translation: no more to sailors grants a passage o ’ er the deep dark main , finding there the heaven ’ s holy bound , upheld by Atlas , where water from ambrosial founts wells up beside the couch of Zeus inside his halls ,

sections.label: Section 1.750
sections.original: ταις , ἵν ’ ἁ βιόδωρος αὔ-
sections.translation: and holy earth , the bounteous mother , causes joy to spring in heavenly breasts .

sections.label: Section 2.752
sections.original: ὦ λευκόπτερε Κρησία
sections.translation: O white-winged bark , that o ’ er the booming ocean-wave didst bring

sections.label: Section 2.755
sections.original: ἐπόρευσας ἐμὰν ἄνασσαν ὀλβίων ἀπ ’ οἴκων ,
sections.translation: my royal mistress from her happy home , to crown her queen ’ mongst sorrow ’ s brides ! Surely evil omens from either port , at least from Crete , were with that ship , what time to glorious Athens it sped its way ,
